ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Mirchi Ka Salan; mapped to the closest parent class 'curry (food product)' as a defensible approximate anchor. A species-level entry for this Hyderabadi chili curry does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Mirchi Ka Salan. Code A0CKP (chilli pepper, cooked dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor given the primary ingredient is large green chilies cooked in a spiced gravy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific regional Indian side dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Mirchi Ka Salan is approximately 150g, typically served as a side dish alongside Hyderabadi biryani at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Mirchi Ka Salan exists in the NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods database. Values are adapted from NIN component data for green chilies, peanuts, sesame seeds, coconut, and tamarind, weighted by typical recipe proportions. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy was used as a secondary cross-reference for peanut and sesame nutrient profiles. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central peanut and sesame oil composition data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mirchi Ka Salan in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on comparable high-fat, high-fibre legume-and-nut-based Indian curry dishes (e.g., peanut-based gravies, dal makhani analogues) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. The high unsaturated fat content from peanuts and sesame, dietary fibre from chilies and coconut, and acidity from tamarind all attenuate postprandial glucose response, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 11.25) / 100 = 3.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (320 mg per 100g) and oil used in frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150g and request reduced salt preparation, as the 320 mg sodium per 100g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets; the unsaturated fats from peanuts and sesame are cardioprotective but oil quantity should be controlled.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) driven by high fat, fibre, and tamarind acidity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mirchi Ka Salan has a low estimated GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of ~3.9 per 150g serving, making it a suitable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions alongside low-GI staples rather than large quantities of white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile from peanuts and sesame seeds; zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is cholesterol-free and derives most of its fat from peanuts and sesame seeds, which are rich in monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids; individuals with cardiovascular disease should nonetheless monitor total fat intake and ensure oil used for frying is not reused or overheated.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds in ginger and turmeric; nightshade sensitivity in a minority of RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger and turmeric in the recipe provide anti-inflammatory benefits; however, green chilies are nightshades and a small subset of RA patients may experience symptom sensitivity and should monitor individual tolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mirchi Ka Salan is a celebrated Hyderabadi side dish from Telangana, India, traditionally served alongside Hyderabadi biryani. The dish features large, mild green chilies — typically the Bhavnagri variety — that are slit, lightly fried until blistered, and then simmered in a deeply flavored, velvety gravy. The gravy base is built from a slow-roasted and ground paste of peanuts, sesame seeds (til), and dry coconut (khopra), which imparts a rich nuttiness and thick, emulsified texture. This nut-and-seed paste is tempered with mustard seeds, fenugreek, curry leaves, and a blend of ginger-garlic paste, then balanced with the sharp sourness of tamarind and warm spices including turmeric, red chili powder, and coriander. The result is a complex, layered gravy that is simultaneously nutty, tangy, mildly spicy, and aromatic. Nutritionally, Mirchi Ka Salan provides approximately 138 kcal per 100g, with 11.2g of predominantly unsaturated fat from peanuts and sesame, 3.8g of protein, and 7.5g of carbohydrate. It is a good source of dietary fibre (2.8g), vitamin C (28mg), iron (1.8mg), and magnesium (42mg). The dish is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 (low) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions. The sodium content of 320mg per 100g warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. Mirchi Ka Salan represents the sophisticated culinary heritage of the Nizami court, fusing Deccan ingredients with Mughlai cooking techniques.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Mirchi Ka Salan exists in the NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN component data for the primary ingredients (green chilies, peanuts, sesame seeds, coconut, tamarind) weighted by typical recipe proportions, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from comparable nut-and-legume-based Indian curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on NIN component ingredient data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-reference; no single approved-source entry for the complete dish exists. 2. Saturated fat (1.8g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.5g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.9g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central peanut and sesame fatty acid profiles weighted by typical recipe proportions. 3. Trans fat is 0.0g as this dish uses unhydrogenated vegetable oils. 4. Cholesterol is 0.0g as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg — confirmed zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero for this food type. 6. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg — naturally zero as this dish contains no vitamin D sources (no fish, eggs, or fortified dairy). 7. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the complete cooked dish is not reported in NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. 8. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable high-fat, high-fibre nut-based Indian curry analogues; no direct GI measurement for Mirchi Ka Salan is published. 9. Glycemic load per serving = (35 × 11.25g carbohydrate per 150g serving) / 100 = 3.94, rounded to 3.9. 10. Portions in the input JSON contained four identical duplicate entries (150g); deduplicated to one canonical serving. 11. The input listed 'Food & Function Journal' as a source for health suitability; this is a peer-reviewed journal and is acceptable for health context support but not used as a primary nutrient source.
